Promise.headline: From the looms of Rajasthan to the floors of the world's most {italic} homes.
Promise.label: Foreword
Promise.body: Each Carpetstory piece is woven by a single artisan, signed by hand, and travels from one room in Jaipur to another, anywhere in the world.

Making.label: II — The making
Making.headline: Eight months of work pass between the first thread and the last. We don't hurry it.
Making.frame1Caption: The Persian knot, tied left over right, repeated a million times.
Making.frame2Alt: Skeins of dyed yarn in shaft of light
Making.frame2Caption: Madder root for the red. Indigo for the blue. Walnut hulls for the brown.
Making.frame2Meta: All natural. All from within 300 km of the workshop.
Making.frame3Alt: A wide shot of a horizontal loom with warp threads strung taut
Making.frame3Caption: One loom. One weaver. Eight months. No assembly line.
Making.frame4Alt: Dye vats, steam rising, hands lifting yarn
Making.frame4Caption: The dyer reads the color by eye. Same recipe, slightly different sky, a different red.
Making.frame4Meta: No two batches are identical. We consider this a feature.
Making.frame5Alt: Yarn drying on rooftops in afternoon sun
Making.frame5Caption: The Rajasthani sun fixes the dye in a way no machine has matched.
Making.frame6Alt: A weaver at scale of the loom, distant
Making.frame6Caption: His name is Salim. His father wove rugs. His grandfather wove rugs. The fourth generation is still deciding.
Making.frame6Meta: Every piece carries the weaver's initials in the selvedge.
Making.frame7Alt: Pile being trimmed with shears, fine clippings falling
Making.frame7Alt2: Detail of clippings collecting on the workshop floor
Making.frame7Caption: The pile is sheared by hand to a millimetre's tolerance.
Making.frame8Alt: Finished rug unrolled on a stone floor
Making.frame8Caption: And then, after eight months — the floor.

Knot.label: III — A study in scale
Knot.hundredSub: One square inch. The weaver works at this density, knot by knot, for eight months.
Knot.tagline: A number that begins to explain the price. And the wait.

Materials.label: IV — The materials
Materials.headline: A short and considered list of things that touch the floor.
Materials.woolDesc: Sourced from highland sheep in Bikaner, where the wool is long-fibered, lanolin-rich, and naturally lustrous. Hand-spun, never machine-carded.
Materials.silkDesc: Mulberry silk drawn from Karnataka. Used as accent threads in the field, where it catches light differently across the day. Reserved for the finer weaves.
Materials.cottonDesc: The warp. The architecture beneath the pile. Long-staple cotton, twisted to a density that holds the knots for a hundred years if cared for.
Materials.dyesHeadline: Three plants. A river. A roof.
Materials.dyesBody: Every colour in a Carpetstory piece begins as a plant, ground or boiled or fermented, then dipped, dried, and dipped again until the wool refuses any more.

ColorStory.label: An aside
ColorStory.headlineEnd: Long before the loom, long before the knot.
ColorStory.footer: Six plants. One river. A roof in the Rajasthani sun. Every colour in a Carpetstory piece begins somewhere you could walk to.

Collection.label: V — The collection
Collection.headline: Six pieces. Chosen, not catalogued.
Collection.khwabDesc: A deep madder field with a single ivory medallion, drawn from a 1920s archive.
Collection.saanjhDesc: Undyed wool, dusk tones, the colour of light at the end of the day.
Collection.mehfilDesc: Indigo and silk. A geometric meeting of straight lines and curved restraint.
Collection.shubhDesc: The auspicious one. Walnut and madder tones, woven as a wedding piece.
Collection.naqshDesc: An architectural piece. Sharp lines, deep colour, designed for considered rooms.
Collection.aaraamDesc: The quiet one. Cream, ivory, the faintest blush. For rooms that ask for stillness.

Heritage.label: VI — Heritage
Heritage.headline: Four generations of keeping the dust out of the dye.
Heritage.p1Body: It started with a single loom in a courtyard in Jaipur. My great-grandfather wove pieces for the local merchants. He was known for a specific madder red that no one else could replicate.
Heritage.p2Body: My father expanded the workshop. We began exporting to Europe, but the rules remained the same: natural dyes, hand-spun wool, and the Persian knot. We refused to adopt the tufting guns that were speeding up the industry.
Heritage.p3Body: Carpetstory is still a family operation. We don't have a factory. We have a network of master weavers, some of whom have worked with our family for three generations. The madder red is still exactly the same.

Letter.label: From the founder
Letter.p1: My grandfather wove. My father sold. I noticed, in between, that the world had stopped looking at the floor.
Letter.p2: Carpetstory is a small attempt to make people look down again. Not at the rug — at the eight months it took, and the hands that took them.

World.label: VII — In the world
World.headline: Where they live.
World.caption1: Khwab in a Brooklyn townhouse — Quincy Architects
World.caption2: Aaraam in a Hampstead drawing room — Studio Iro
World.caption3: Shubh in a Marylebone apartment — Private client
World.caption4: Naqsh in a Parisian pied-à-terre — Studio Marais

Testimonials.label: VIII — Voices
Testimonials.q1: It arrived in a wooden crate that smelled of cedar. The rug smelled of wool and sun. Eight months later, both still do.
Testimonials.q2: We have one Carpetstory in the dining room. Guests always sit on the floor.
Testimonials.q3: You don't buy a piece like this. You inherit it forward.
Testimonials.q4: I asked Aashrit for something the colour of late afternoon. What arrived was exactly that.
Testimonials.q5: The pile is so dense your foot sinks a quarter inch. I notice it every morning.
Testimonials.q6: Specified for a client in Geneva. They wrote a year later just to say the rug had aged better than the room around it.
Testimonials.q7: It looks like something you'd find in a museum and want to take home. We took it home.
Testimonials.q8: The corner curls slightly where the loom ended. I love that. A machine wouldn't have left that.
Testimonials.q9: Eight months is a long time to wait for a floor. Forty years is a long time to keep one. We're betting on the second number.

Doors.leftBody: Tell us about the room, the light, the way the day moves through it. We'll suggest a piece, or commission a new one.
Doors.rightBody: Trade pricing, full collection access, project samples, and a dedicated point of contact for specification.

TwoDoors.tradeBody: Access our trade portal for tear sheets, trade pricing, and custom commissions.
TwoDoors.commissionBody: Speak directly with our atelier about sourcing a piece from the archive or commissioning a custom size.

Inquiry.imaginingPlaceholder: A piece for the living room, somewhere between madder and rust. The room faces west and the light is generous in the afternoon…
